title: Eve in Islam and Christianity
subtitle: Two tellings of the first disobedience — who is blamed, what is inherited, and what each structure has meant for women
summary: Genesis narrates a fall in which the woman acts first, and later Christian texts — from 1 Timothy to Tertullian — built arguments about all women on that priority, arguments most churches today qualify or reject. The Quran retells the same primal scene in the grammatical dual: Satan whispers to the couple together, both eat, both repent, both are forgiven, and the one verse of individual reproach names Adam. This article quotes the core texts of both traditions and lays out the structural differences — inherited condition versus clean slate, woman-first versus joint address — without declaring a winner.

seo.metaTitle: Eve in Islam and Christianity: Who Was Blamed for the Fall?
seo.metaDescription: How Genesis 3, 1 Timothy 2, and Tertullian shaped the Christian reading of Eve — and how the Quran addresses the fall to Adam and his wife jointly, with both forgiven and nothing inherited.
keywords: Adam and Eve in the Quran

sections.title: Why Eve Is the Starting Point
sections.body:
No single figure has shaped the two traditions' conversations about women more than the first woman herself — Eve in the Bible, Hawwa in Islamic tradition (named in hadith literature, though not named in the Quran). In Christian history, Genesis 3, read through 1 Timothy 2 and amplified by some early church writers, became the story of the woman who was deceived first — and, in the harshest patristic rhetoric, the story of a guilt her daughters share. In the Quran, the same primal scene is retold with a different grammar: Satan whispers to the couple together, both eat, both repent, both are forgiven, and the one verse of individual reproach names Adam.

This article works through the primary texts in order. It quotes the Genesis narrative and the key New Testament passages; it quotes, verbatim and in context, the most severe historical statement of the Eve-blaming reading and the mainstream Christian responses to it; it then quotes the Quranic tellings and deals honestly with the Eve-negative material that exists in later Islamic literature. It closes by stating the structural differences precisely. Whether one structure is truer or better than the other is left to the reader; what the texts actually say is not left vague.

sections.title: The Genesis Narrative: The Woman Acts First
sections.body:
Genesis 3 opens with the serpent questioning the woman about God's command concerning the tree. The eating itself takes a single verse: “When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was to be desired to make one wise, she took some of its fruit, and ate; and she gave some to her husband with her, and he ate it, too.” (Genesis 3:6, World English Bible). Two details here have carried enormous interpretive weight. The woman acts first — she sees, takes, eats, and gives. And the phrase “her husband with her” has been read by many commentators to mean Adam was present throughout the exchange, a detail that complicates later retellings in which Eve seeks out and seduces an absent Adam.

When God questions the pair, each deflects blame downward: “The man said, “The woman whom you gave to be with me, she gave me fruit from the tree, and I ate it.” Yahweh God said to the woman, “What have you done?” The woman said, “The serpent deceived me, and I ate.”” (Genesis 3:12-13). God then pronounces consequences on the serpent, the woman, and the man in sequence. The address to the woman is the verse that has echoed longest: “To the woman he said, “I will greatly multiply your pain in childbirth. In pain you will bear children. Your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you.”” (Genesis 3:16). Pain in childbearing and the husband's rule enter the story here, as words spoken to the woman within the judgment scene. The man's address follows in the same scene: the ground is cursed on his account and his labor becomes toil until he returns to dust.

What Genesis 3 itself does not say is also important for what follows: it contains no statement that the woman's descendants inherit her act, and no ranking of the two transgressions. Those moves came later, in different directions, in both the New Testament and post-biblical interpretation.

sections.title: Two New Testament Trajectories: Eve Deceived, Sin Through Adam
sections.body:
Two New Testament passages fixed the terms of the later Christian debate, and they pull in different directions.

The first is from the first letter to Timothy: “Let a woman learn in quietness with full submission. But I don’t permit a woman to teach, nor to exercise authority over a man, but to be in quietness. For Adam was first formed, then Eve. Adam wasn’t deceived, but the woman, being deceived, has fallen into disobedience;” (1 Timothy 2:11-14). The passage grounds a restriction on women's teaching and authority in two claims about Eden — Adam was formed first, and it was the woman, not Adam, who was deceived. For much of Christian history this text was read as establishing a permanent order between the sexes, with Eve's deception as standing evidence.

The second passage runs the other way. When Paul explains in Romans how sin and death entered the world, he names one person, and it is not Eve: “Therefore as sin entered into the world through one man, and death through sin; and so death passed to all men, because all sinned.” (Romans 5:12). The fall-theology of Romans 5 is built entirely on Adam — sin enters “through one man,” and Christ is presented as the counterpart of Adam. When Christian doctrine later formalized the transmission of the fall as original sin, it followed Romans and made Adam, not Eve, the channel.

Both strands are canonical. Which one governs the reading of the other is among the questions that still divides Christian interpreters, and it matters directly for the question this article asks: whether anything from Eden attaches to women as women.

sections.title: The Patristic Legacy: Tertullian’s Indictment and Irenaeus’s Reversal
sections.body:
The harshest surviving statement of the Eve-blaming reading comes from Tertullian of Carthage (c. 155–c. 220), writing around the turn of the third century in a treatise on women's dress. Addressing the Christian women of his congregation, he wrote: “And do you not know that you are (each) an Eve? The sentence of God on this sex of yours lives in this age: the guilt must of necessity live too. You are the devil's gateway: you are the unsealer of that (forbidden) tree: you are the first deserter of the divine law: you are she who persuaded him whom the devil was not valiant enough to attack. You destroyed so easily God's image, man. On account of your desert — that is, death — even the Son of God had to die.” (On the Apparel of Women, Book I, ch. 1, trans. S. Thelwall, Ante-Nicene Fathers vol. 4). The logic is explicit and total: every woman is an Eve; God's sentence on “this sex” lives on, and so does the guilt; and even the death of the Son of God is laid at woman's door. Other early writers argued in a similar register — John Chrysostom's homilies on 1 Timothy, for example, treat Eve's deception as evidence of a weakness shared by the sex — though the fathers were not unanimous in this.

The patristic record also contains the opposite motif, and it is older than Tertullian's treatise. Irenaeus of Lyons set Eve beside Mary as parallel and reversal: “And thus also it was that the knot of Eve's disobedience was loosed by the obedience of Mary.” (Against Heresies, Book III, ch. 22, sec. 4). In this strand, which became central to Catholic and Orthodox devotion, Eve's story is answered rather than perpetuated: Mary is honored as the “new Eve” whose obedience unties what disobedience knotted. Both motifs — woman as the devil's gateway and woman as the agent of reversal — descend from the same Genesis text, through different interpretive choices.

sections.title: How Mainstream Christianity Answers the “Devil’s Gateway” Reading
sections.body:
Fairness requires stating the mainstream Christian responses at full strength, because on this topic they are substantial.

First, Tertullian is not scripture. No ecumenical council adopted his rhetoric; he ended his life aligned with the Montanist movement, and no major church confesses that women bear Eve's guilt as women. His words are quoted today mostly as a warning specimen of how far one reading could go.

Second, where churches do teach a transmitted fall, the channel is Adam and the content is carefully qualified. The Catechism of the Catholic Church (secs. 404-405) teaches that original sin is “contracted,” not “committed” — a deprived state of human nature that, in its own words, does not have the character of a personal fault in Adam's descendants. Eastern Orthodoxy speaks of ancestral sin: what humanity inherits is mortality and a damaged condition, not guilt. Reformed confessions retain a stronger doctrine of imputed guilt — but imputed from Adam, following Romans 5:12, not from Eve.

Third, much modern exegesis, including official Catholic teaching, reads Genesis 3:16 as description rather than prescription. On this reading, “he will rule over you” describes the disordered condition that follows the rupture between humanity and God — a distortion redemption works against, not a norm God endorses. John Paul II's apostolic letter Mulieris Dignitatem (1988, sec. 10) argues exactly this: the domination of the words “he shall rule over you” breaks the original equality of man and woman and diminishes both.

Fourth, 1 Timothy 2:11-14 is contested within Christianity itself. Complementarian interpreters take its appeal to creation order as a permanent arrangement for church teaching offices, while explicitly denying that it implies women's inherited guilt or lesser worth. Egalitarian interpreters locate the instruction in a specific first-century Ephesian situation — unlearned women and circulating false teaching — and point to the women Paul greets as co-workers and a deacon in the greetings of Romans 16. Many churches today ordain women; many others do not; both camps claim the New Testament.

Finally, Mary. In Catholic and Orthodox thought the most consequential woman in the story of salvation is not Eve but the new Eve, and the tradition's liturgical instinct — honoring a woman as higher than the angels — is itself offered as the answer to Tertullian's rhetoric.

sections.title: The Quranic Narrative: Told in the Dual
sections.body:
The Quran tells the Eden story in several surahs, and its most consistent feature is grammatical. Arabic possesses a dedicated dual form — verbs and pronouns that refer to exactly two people — and the Quranic fall is narrated almost entirely in it.

In Surah al-Baqara the command is given to both at once: “And We said, "O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise and eat therefrom in [ease and] abundance from wherever you will. But do not approach this tree, lest you be among the wrongdoers."” (Quran 2:35). The fall likewise happens to both: “But Satan caused them to slip out of it and removed them from that [condition] in which they had been. And We said, "Go down, [all of you], as enemies to one another, and you will have upon the earth a place of settlement and provision for a time."” (Quran 2:36).

Surah al-A'raf sustains the dual through the entire temptation. “But Satan whispered to them to make apparent to them that which was concealed from them of their private parts. He said, "Your Lord did not forbid you this tree except that you become angels or become of the immortal."” (Quran 7:20). “And he swore [by Allah] to them, "Indeed, I am to you from among the sincere advisors."” (Quran 7:21). “So he made them fall, through deception. And when they tasted of the tree, their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten together over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And their Lord called to them, "Did I not forbid you from that tree and tell you that Satan is to you a clear enemy?"” (Quran 7:22). Satan whispers to them both, swears to them both, deceives them both; they taste together, are exposed together, and are called to account together — “Did I not forbid you” is addressed, in the Arabic dual, to the two of them.

At no point in the Quran does the woman eat first, tempt her husband, or receive a separate address of blame or punishment. The tempter is Satan; the tempted are the pair. The Quran also never names her — Hawwa is her name in hadith and tafsir literature — and never uses her as an argument about other women.

sections.title: Reproach Falls on Adam; Both Repent; Both Are Forgiven
sections.body:
Where the Quran does single out one of the two, the singling-out runs in the opposite direction from 1 Timothy. In Surah Ta-Ha the forgotten covenant is Adam's: “And We had already taken a promise from Adam before, but he forgot; and We found not in him determination.” (Quran 20:115). After narrating that both ate and both began covering themselves, the verse of individual censure names him alone: “And Adam and his wife ate of it, and their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred.” (Quran 20:121). And the resolution follows in the very next verse: “Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him].” (Quran 20:122).

In Surah al-A'raf the repentance is explicitly joint, spoken in the dual voice: “They said, "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers."” (Quran 7:23). The story thus closes inside itself — disobedience, joint repentance, acceptance, guidance. The descent to earth is not narrated as an unhealed curse: Quran 2:36 pairs it with “a place of settlement and provision for a time,” and Quran 20:122 with being chosen, forgiven, and guided. In Islamic theology Adam is not only forgiven but becomes the first prophet.

The contrast in narrative mechanics is worth stating neutrally: Genesis 3 ends with expulsion and consequences pronounced; the arc of repair unfolds across the rest of the Christian Bible and centers on Christ. The Quranic tellings compress fall, repentance, and forgiveness into the same passage, so that no unresolved debt leaves Eden with the couple.

sections.title: No Inherited Burden, and Childbirth Reframed
sections.body:
Islamic theology draws a doctrinal line under this narrative closure: nothing of the episode transmits to anyone. The Quran states it as a general principle of moral accounting: “And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And if a heavily laden soul calls [another] to [carry some of] its load, nothing of it will be carried, even if he should be a close relative. You can only warn those who fear their Lord unseen and have established prayer. And whoever purifies himself only purifies himself for [the benefit of] his soul. And to Allah is the [final] destination.” (Quran 35:18) — a principle repeated in similar words in several other surahs. Sunni and Shia theology alike hold that every human being is born morally clean; a well-known hadith in Sahih al-Bukhari states that every child is born upon the fitra, the innate God-given disposition. There is, accordingly, no Islamic doctrine of original sin, no baptism to remove an inherited stain, and no category of guilt attaching to women as daughters of Eve.

Childbirth pain — which Genesis 3:16 places inside the judgment addressed to the woman — appears in the Quran in a different register entirely. “And We have enjoined upon man, to his parents, good treatment. His mother carried him with hardship and gave birth to him with hardship, and his gestation and weaning [period] is thirty months. [He grows] until, when he reaches maturity and reaches [the age of] forty years, he says, "My Lord, enable me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents and to work righteousness of which You will approve and make righteous for me my offspring. Indeed, I have repented to You, and indeed, I am of the Muslims."” (Quran 46:15). The hardship of pregnancy and labor is cited here as the ground of a lifelong debt of gratitude the child owes the mother, and Islamic tradition builds the mother's elevated status partly on this verse. No Quranic verse connects the pain of labor to Eve, to Eden, or to punishment.

On the specific question this article asks — does anything from the first disobedience follow all women? — the Quranic answer is structural, not incidental: the fall is joint, the repentance is joint, the forgiveness is explicit, and the accounting of every later human being starts at zero.

sections.title: An Honest Caveat: Eve in Hadith and Tafsir
sections.body:
Honesty requires a caveat on the Islamic side, and a Christian reader is entitled to press it. While the Quran never blames Eve alone, some later Islamic literature does contain Eve-negative material. A hadith in Sahih al-Bukhari (no. 3330; a close variant appears in Sahih Muslim) reads, in the USC-MSA English translation: “Narrated Abu Huraira: The Prophet said, 'But for the Israelis, meat would not decay and but for Eve, wives would never betray their husbands.'” Classical tafsir works — al-Tabari's commentary among them — also transmit narratives in which Eve tempts Adam, generally paralleling Genesis and later Jewish elaborations; Muslim scholarship labels much of this imported narrative material Isra'iliyyat and has long treated it with reserve, since it derives from earlier communities' traditions rather than from the Quran or the Prophet.

How Muslims read the Bukhari hadith varies. Classical commentators generally took it as an observation that Eve set a precedent her daughters sometimes follow — betrayal here being read as concealment or acting against a husband's wishes, on the model of Eve encouraging Adam to eat — rather than as a doctrine that women inherit her guilt; and it is a fact of Islamic law and creed that no ruling about women's nature or standing was ever derived from it. Modern academic work, notably Barbara Freyer Stowasser's Women in the Qur'an, Traditions, and Interpretation (1994), documents both sides of this record: the Quran's strictly joint narrative, and the entry of Eve-blaming motifs into hadith commentary and tafsir through post-Quranic channels.

The fair symmetry should be stated plainly. Just as Christians distinguish scripture from Tertullian, Muslims distinguish the Quran from transmitted reports and exegetical folklore. This article therefore draws its comparison where each tradition locates its highest textual authority — Genesis and the New Testament on one side, the Quran on the other — while acknowledging that both traditions' secondary literatures absorbed harsher material about Eve than their scriptures required.

sections.title: The Structural Comparison, Stated Precisely
sections.body:
Set side by side, the foundational texts differ at four precise points.

1. Who is blamed in the primal scene. In Genesis 3:6 the woman sees, takes, eats, and gives; the judgment words addressed to her in Genesis 3:16 include childbirth pain and her husband's rule; and 1 Timothy 2:11-14 later builds an argument about women's roles on her having been deceived. Yet Romans 5:12 assigns the entry of sin to Adam, and formal Christian doctrine followed Romans. In the Quran the narration is dual throughout (2:35-36; 7:20-23), and where one party is singled out for reproach it is Adam (20:115; 20:121).

2. What is inherited. Classical Western Christianity teaches a transmitted condition: imputed guilt in Augustinian and Reformed formulations, a “contracted” deprivation that is not personal fault in current Catholic teaching, inherited mortality and corruption in Orthodoxy. Islam teaches that nothing transmits: no bearer of burdens bears the burden of another (35:18), and every child is born clean.

3. Childbirth. Genesis 3:16 places the pain of childbearing inside the judgment scene, addressed to the woman. Quran 46:15 presents the same pain as the mother's merit and the child's debt, with no connection to Eden.

4. The repair. Christianity answers Eve outside Eden — through Christ, the counterpart of Adam in Romans 5, and through Mary, honored as the new Eve. Islam answers the fall inside the story itself: both repent (7:23), forgiveness is granted (20:122), and the account is closed before human history begins.

Two caveats keep the comparison honest. Christianity is not monolithic: inherited guilt in the strict sense is denied by Orthodoxy and carefully qualified by Rome; many churches read Genesis 3:16 as description of a broken condition rather than divine mandate; and no major church today teaches Tertullian's claim that women share Eve's guilt. And the Islamic tradition is not spotless in practice: Eve-blaming motifs entered tafsir, and a sound hadith links wives' betrayal to Eve's precedent. But at the level of the founding texts — the level each tradition itself treats as decisive — the structures genuinely differ: a narrative in which the woman moves first, on which later canonical and post-canonical texts built durable arguments about women; and a narrative told in the dual, with individual reproach directed at the man, joint repentance, and a slate wiped clean. What follows from each structure for women's actual lives has been argued in both directions for centuries. That argument is the reader's to weigh; the texts above are its raw material.

sections.title: Sources and How to Verify Them
sections.body:
Bible quotations (Genesis 3:6; 3:12-13; 3:16; 1 Timothy 2:11-14; Romans 5:12) are from the World English Bible, fetched from bible-api.com; the WEB is a public-domain modern translation. Quran quotations (2:35-36; 7:20-23; 20:115; 20:121-122; 35:18; 46:15) are from api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani Arabic text with the Saheeh International English translation.

Tertullian, On the Apparel of Women (De cultu feminarum), Book I, chapter 1, is quoted from the S. Thelwall translation in Ante-Nicene Fathers, volume 4; the text is freely available at newadvent.org (fathers/0402.htm) and at tertullian.org. Irenaeus, Against Heresies, Book III, chapter 22, section 4, is quoted from Ante-Nicene Fathers, volume 1, at newadvent.org (fathers/0103322.htm).

Sahih al-Bukhari 3330 is quoted in the USC-MSA English translation (Volume 4, Book 55, Number 547); the reference on sunnah.com is bukhari:3330. The Catechism of the Catholic Church, sections 404-405, and John Paul II's Mulieris Dignitatem, section 10, are paraphrased rather than quoted; both documents are available in full at vatican.va. Readers are encouraged to check every quotation in this article against these sources directly.

quranVerses.arabic: وَقُلْنَا يَٰٓـَٔادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَكُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ
quranVerses.translation: And We said, "O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise and eat therefrom in [ease and] abundance from wherever you will. But do not approach this tree, lest you be among the wrongdoers."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَأَزَلَّهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَٰنُ عَنْهَا فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ ۖ وَقُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّۭ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّۭ وَمَتَٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍۢ
quranVerses.translation: But Satan caused them to slip out of it and removed them from that [condition] in which they had been. And We said, "Go down, [all of you], as enemies to one another, and you will have upon the earth a place of settlement and provision for a time."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَٰنُ لِيُبْدِىَ لَهُمَا مَا وُۥرِىَ عَنْهُمَا مِن سَوْءَٰتِهِمَا وَقَالَ مَا نَهَىٰكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلْخَٰلِدِينَ
quranVerses.translation: But Satan whispered to them to make apparent to them that which was concealed from them of their private parts. He said, "Your Lord did not forbid you this tree except that you become angels or become of the immortal."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: And he swore [by Allah] to them, "Indeed, I am to you from among the sincere advisors."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَدَلَّىٰهُمَا بِغُرُورٍۢ ۚ فَلَمَّا ذَاقَا ٱلشَّجَرَةَ بَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ وَنَادَىٰهُمَا رَبُّهُمَآ أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا ٱلشَّجَرَةِ وَأَقُل لَّكُمَآ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَٰنَ لَكُمَا عَدُوٌّۭ مُّبِينٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: So he made them fall, through deception. And when they tasted of the tree, their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten together over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And their Lord called to them, "Did I not forbid you from that tree and tell you that Satan is to you a clear enemy?"
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: قَالَا رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَآ أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَٰسِرِينَ
quranVerses.translation: They said, "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِىَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُۥ عَزْمًۭا
quranVerses.translation: And We had already taken a promise from Adam before, but he forgot; and We found not in him determination.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ وَعَصَىٰٓ ءَادَمُ رَبَّهُۥ فَغَوَىٰ
quranVerses.translation: And Adam and his wife ate of it, and their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ثُمَّ ٱجْتَبَٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ
quranVerses.translation: Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him].
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَإِن تَدْعُ مُثْقَلَةٌ إِلَىٰ حِمْلِهَا لَا يُحْمَلْ مِنْهُ شَىْءٌۭ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰٓ ۗ إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ وَمَن تَزَكَّىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَتَزَكَّىٰ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ
quranVerses.translation: And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And if a heavily laden soul calls [another] to [carry some of] its load, nothing of it will be carried, even if he should be a close relative. You can only warn those who fear their Lord unseen and have established prayer. And whoever purifies himself only purifies himself for [the benefit of] his soul. And to Allah is the [final] destination.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ إِحْسَٰنًا ۖ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ كُرْهًۭا وَوَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهًۭا ۖ وَحَمْلُهُۥ وَفِصَٰلُهُۥ ثَلَٰثُونَ شَهْرًا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةًۭ قَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِىٓ أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَىَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَٰلِحًۭا تَرْضَىٰهُ وَأَصْلِحْ لِى فِى ذُرِّيَّتِىٓ ۖ إِنِّى تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَإِنِّى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ
quranVerses.translation: And We have enjoined upon man, to his parents, good treatment. His mother carried him with hardship and gave birth to him with hardship, and his gestation and weaning [period] is thirty months. [He grows] until, when he reaches maturity and reaches [the age of] forty years, he says, "My Lord, enable me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents and to work righteousness of which You will approve and make righteous for me my offspring. Indeed, I have repented to You, and indeed, I am of the Muslims."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was to be desired to make one wise, she took some of its fruit, and ate; and she gave some to her husband with her, and he ate it, too.

The man said, “The woman whom you gave to be with me, she gave me fruit from the tree, and I ate it.” Yahweh God said to the woman, “What have you done?” The woman said, “The serpent deceived me, and I ate.”
To the woman he said, “I will greatly multiply your pain in childbirth. In pain you will bear children. Your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you.”

Let a woman learn in quietness with full submission. But I don’t permit a woman to teach, nor to exercise authority over a man, but to be in quietness. For Adam was first formed, then Eve. Adam wasn’t deceived, but the woman, being deceived, has fallen into disobedience;
Therefore as sin entered into the world through one man, and death through sin; and so death passed to all men, because all sinned.

faq.question: Does Christianity teach that all women share Eve’s guilt?
faq.answer: No major church teaches this as doctrine. Tertullian wrote that every woman is “an Eve” and that “the guilt must of necessity live too,” but that is a church father’s rhetoric, not scripture or creed. Where churches teach a transmitted fall (original sin), the channel is Adam, following Romans 5:12, and current Catholic teaching states that original sin is not a personal fault in any descendant; Orthodoxy teaches inherited mortality rather than guilt. Some churches do restrict women’s teaching roles based on 1 Timothy 2, but that position is argued from creation order, not from inherited guilt, and it is contested by other churches.

faq.question: Does the Quran blame Eve for the fall?
faq.answer: No verse in the Quran blames Eve alone or first. The narrations in Surah al-Baqara (2:35-36) and Surah al-A’raf (7:20-23) are in the Arabic dual form throughout: Satan whispers to them both, both eat, both are called to account, and both repent together. Where the Quran singles out one person for reproach, it is Adam: “And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred” (20:121), followed immediately by his being forgiven and guided (20:122). The Quran also never uses the episode as an argument about women in general.

faq.question: Do Muslims believe in original sin?
faq.answer: No. The Quran states that “no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another” (35:18), and in the Quranic narrative Adam and his wife repent and are explicitly forgiven (7:23; 20:122), so there is no unresolved debt to transmit. Islamic theology holds that every child is born on the fitra — an innate clean disposition — which is why Islam has no rite equivalent to baptism for removing an inherited stain. Each person answers only for what he or she does.

faq.question: Is childbirth pain a punishment for Eve’s sin in Islam?
faq.answer: No. Genesis 3:16 places multiplied pain in childbearing inside God’s judgment addressed to the woman, and that framing shaped centuries of Christian interpretation — though many modern Christian readings treat the verse as describing a broken condition rather than prescribing punishment. The Quran mentions the pain of pregnancy and birth in Surah al-Ahqaf (46:15), where it functions as the basis of the mother’s claim to her child’s lifelong gratitude. No Quranic verse links labor pain to Eve or to Eden.

faq.question: Doesn’t Islamic tradition also contain negative material about Eve?
faq.answer: Yes, and honest comparison requires saying so. A hadith in Sahih al-Bukhari (3330) says that “but for Eve, wives would never betray their husbands,” and classical tafsir transmitted Eve-tempts-Adam narratives that scholars classify as Isra’iliyyat — material absorbed from earlier traditions rather than from the Quran. Classical commentators generally read the hadith as noting a precedent rather than an inherited guilt, and no creedal or legal ruling about women was built on it. The parallel is instructive: Muslims distinguish the Quran from later literature much as Christians distinguish scripture from Tertullian.

faq.question: What does it mean that Mary is called the “new Eve”?
faq.answer: From the second century, Christian writers set Mary beside Eve as reversal: Irenaeus wrote that “the knot of Eve’s disobedience was loosed by the obedience of Mary” (Against Heresies III.22.4). In Catholic and Orthodox thought this became a central devotional theme — as death entered through one woman’s disobedience, the Redeemer entered through another woman’s obedience. Defenders of Christianity point to this strand, and to Mary’s unmatched liturgical honor, as evidence that the tradition’s considered response to Eve is not blame but reversal.

furtherReading.title: Eve and Adam: Jewish, Christian, and Muslim Readings on Genesis and Gender
furtherReading.author: Kristen E. Kvam, Linda S. Schearing, and Valarie H. Ziegler (eds.)
furtherReading.note: An anthology of primary sources from all three traditions on Eve and the fall, letting readers compare the interpretive history side by side (Indiana University Press, 1999).

furtherReading.title: Women in the Qur’an, Traditions, and Interpretation
furtherReading.author: Barbara Freyer Stowasser
furtherReading.note: The standard academic study of how the Quran’s joint fall-narrative differs from later hadith and tafsir material on Eve (Oxford University Press, 1994).

furtherReading.title: Adam, Eve, and the Serpent
furtherReading.author: Elaine Pagels
furtherReading.note: A historian’s account of how early Christians, from Paul to Augustine, transformed the Genesis story and built doctrines of sin and sexuality on it (Random House, 1988).

furtherReading.title: God and the Rhetoric of Sexuality
furtherReading.author: Phyllis Trible
furtherReading.note: A close literary reading of Genesis 2-3 arguing that the text describes rather than prescribes male rule; influential in modern Christian exegesis of Genesis 3:16 (Fortress Press, 1978).
